title: Apache Kafka Complete Guide
description: A comprehensive reference guide covering Apache Kafka from fundamentals to advanced topics, with code examples and interview preparation.

topics.name: What is Apache Kafka
topics.explanation: Apache Kafka is a distributed, fault-tolerant, high-throughput event streaming platform originally developed at LinkedIn in 2011 and later open-sourced through the Apache Software Foundation. At its core, Kafka acts as a distributed commit log — an append-only, ordered, immutable sequence of records. It is designed to handle trillions of events per day with low latency and high durability. Kafka decouples data producers from consumers, enabling asynchronous, scalable communication between microservices, data pipelines, and analytics systems.
key_concepts: Distributed commit log — append-only, durable, ordered record storage
Publish-Subscribe model — producers write to topics, consumers read from topics
Horizontal scalability — scale by adding brokers and partitions
Durability — messages are persisted to disk and replicated across brokers
Fault tolerance — continues operating even when brokers fail
Stream processing — real-time transformation via Kafka Streams or ksqlDB

example.description: Basic Kafka Producer and Consumer in Python using confluent-kafka

topics.name: Kafka Use Cases
topics.explanation: Kafka serves as the backbone for a wide range of real-time and batch workloads across industries. Its versatility comes from its ability to act as both a messaging system and a storage system.

use_cases.name: Real-Time Event Streaming
use_cases.description: Stream user clickstreams, IoT sensor data, or financial transactions in real time for immediate processing and analytics.

use_cases.name: Log Aggregation
use_cases.description: Centralize application logs from thousands of microservices into a single Kafka topic for downstream processing by Elasticsearch or Splunk.

use_cases.name: Change Data Capture (CDC)
use_cases.description: Capture database changes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) using Debezium and stream them to downstream systems, keeping data in sync.

use_cases.name: Microservices Communication
use_cases.description: Decouple microservices using Kafka as an asynchronous message bus, eliminating tight coupling and improving resilience.

use_cases.name: Stream Processing Pipelines
use_cases.description: Build real-time ETL pipelines that transform, enrich, and aggregate data before loading it into data warehouses or data lakes.

use_cases.name: Event Sourcing
use_cases.description: Store every state change as an immutable event in Kafka, enabling complete audit trails and state reconstruction.

use_cases.name: Metrics & Monitoring
use_cases.description: Collect operational metrics from systems and push them to monitoring tools like Prometheus and Grafana.

topics.name: Kafka vs Traditional Messaging Systems (RabbitMQ, ActiveMQ)
topics.explanation: Traditional message brokers like RabbitMQ and ActiveMQ use a push model where the broker manages message delivery and deletion after consumption. Kafka uses a pull model with a durable, indexed log.

comparison_table.kafka: Millions of messages/sec (sequential disk I/O, batching)
Pull-based; consumers track their own offsets
Yes — consumers can reset offsets and re-read
High-throughput streaming, event sourcing, log aggregation

topics.name: Kafka vs Database
topics.explanation: Kafka is not a replacement for a traditional database, but it shares some database-like characteristics due to its durable log structure.

kafka_strengths: Ordered, immutable log of events — great for event sourcing
Built-in pub/sub fanout to many consumers
Log compaction simulates key-value store behavior
database_strengths: Random reads and writes by primary key
ACID transactions at the row level
comparison.when_to_use_kafka: Use Kafka when you need to stream events between systems, process data in real time, or build an audit trail. Use a database when you need queryable, mutable state.

topics.name: Event Streaming vs Message Queue
topics.explanation: Understanding the difference is fundamental to choosing the right tool.

event_streaming.definition: A continuous, durable log of events that multiple consumers can read independently and replay at will.
characteristics: Multiple independent consumer groups each get all messages

message_queue.definition: A buffer where a message is consumed by exactly one consumer and then deleted.
characteristics: Load balancing — each message goes to one consumer
Better for task distribution and RPC patterns

topics.name: Kafka Ecosystem Overview
topics.explanation: The Kafka ecosystem has grown to include a rich set of components that together form a complete stream processing platform.

components.name: Apache Kafka Core
components.role: Distributed event log — brokers, producers, consumers

components.name: Kafka Connect
components.role: Framework for integrating Kafka with external systems (DBs, S3, Elasticsearch) using source/sink connectors

components.name: Kafka Streams
components.role: Client library for building stateful stream processing applications directly on Kafka

components.name: ksqlDB
components.role: SQL-based stream processing engine built on top of Kafka Streams

components.name: Schema Registry
components.role: Centralized schema management for Avro, Protobuf, and JSON Schema

components.name: MirrorMaker 2
components.role: Kafka-to-Kafka replication for multi-region/disaster recovery setups

components.name: Confluent Platform
components.role: Enterprise distribution with Control Center, RBAC, tiered storage, and more

components.name: Strimzi / Confluent Operator
components.role: Kubernetes operators for deploying and managing Kafka clusters on K8s

timeline.event: Kafka developed at LinkedIn by Jay Kreps, Neha Narkhede, and Jun Rao. Open-sourced via Apache.
Apache Kafka becomes a top-level Apache project.
Confluent founded by the original Kafka creators.
Kafka Streams introduced (0.10.x), enabling stream processing without external frameworks.
Exactly-Once Semantics (EOS) introduced in Kafka 0.11 — a major milestone.
ksqlDB (originally KSQL) released for SQL-based stream processing.
KIP-500 proposed — roadmap to remove ZooKeeper dependency.
KRaft (Kafka Raft Metadata) mode introduced in early access (Kafka 2.8).
KRaft mode production-ready in Kafka 3.3. ZooKeeper deprecated.
Kafka 3.5+ — ZooKeeper mode removal timeline announced. KRaft becomes the default.
Kafka 3.7/3.8 — Full KRaft maturity, new consumer group protocol (KIP-848) in preview.

sections.title: Kafka Architecture

topics.name: Brokers
topics.explanation: A Kafka broker is a highly optimized server process that acts as the backbone of the cluster. It is responsible for receiving messages from producers, storing them securely as log segments on local disk, and serving them to consumers. Brokers are designed for massive throughput, leveraging sequential I/O and 'Zero-Copy' memory transfer (sendfile) to move data directly from the OS page cache to the network buffer, bypassing the JVM heap.
topics.diagram:
graph LR
    P1[Producer 1] --> B1[Broker 1]
    P2[Producer 2] --> B2[Broker 2]
    subgraph Kafka Cluster
    B1 <--> B2
    B2 <--> B3[Broker 3]
    end
    B1 --> C1[Consumer 1]
    B3 --> C2[Consumer 2]
topics.pro_tip: Always monitor the 'Under-Replicated Partitions' (URP) metric. A non-zero URP is the most critical health indicator, suggesting that one or more brokers are struggling to keep up with the leader's replication requests.
topics.warning: Avoid over-partitioning a single broker. Each partition creates file handles and consumes RAM for the index and offset cache. A single broker should typically not manage more than 2,000-4,000 partitions for optimal performance.
key_properties: Each broker is identified by broker.id (integer)
Stores topic partitions as log segments on local disk
Serves as leader for some partitions and follower for others
Handles producer writes and consumer fetches
One broker per cluster is elected as the Controller

topics.name: Clusters
topics.explanation: A Kafka cluster is a group of brokers working together. Clusters provide horizontal scalability and fault tolerance. Partitions are distributed across brokers so that no single broker is a bottleneck. Replication ensures data is available even if brokers fail. Production clusters typically have 3–12 brokers; large deployments (Netflix, LinkedIn) run thousands.

topics.name: Topics & Partitions: The Engine of Scale
topics.explanation:
### Deep Dive into Kafka's Data Model

Kafka's ability to scale horizontally is entirely dependent on its use of **Topics** and **Partitions**. While a topic is a logical category, partitions are the physical unit of parallelism and storage.

#### 1. Topics: The Logical Stream
*   **Definition**: A named stream of records. Analogous to a table in a database or a folder in a filesystem.
*   **Immutability**: Once a record is written to a topic, it cannot be changed. It is appended to the end of the log.
*   **Retention**: Data is kept for a configurable period (default 7 days) or until a size limit is reached.

#### 2. Partitions: The Parallel Unit
*   **Scale-Out**: A single topic can be split into multiple partitions. These partitions can be distributed across different brokers in the cluster.
*   **Parallelism**: Each partition can be read by only ONE consumer in a consumer group at a time. Therefore, the number of partitions determines your maximum consumer parallelism.
*   **Ordering**: Kafka guarantees **Strict Ordering** within a single partition. Across partitions, there is no global ordering.

#### 3. The Offset
*   Each record in a partition is assigned a unique, sequential ID called an **Offset**.
*   Consumers track their progress by 'committing' the last offset they successfully processed.
topics.diagram:
graph TD
    A[Topic: orders] --> B[Partition 0]
    A --> C[Partition 1]
    A --> D[Partition 2]
    B --> B1[Broker 1]
    C --> C1[Broker 2]
    D --> D1[Broker 3]
    subgraph Consumer Group
    B1 --> E[Consumer A]
    C1 --> F[Consumer B]
    D1 --> G[Consumer C]
    end
topics.pro_tip: **Choose your Partition Key wisely.** By default, Kafka uses a round-robin strategy (if key is null) or a hash of the key. To ensure all events for a specific user or order stay in chronological order, use the `user_id` or `order_id` as the message key.
topics.warning: **Do not over-partition.** While more partitions increase parallelism, they also increase file handles and latency during broker failover. A good rule of thumb is to aim for < 4,000 partitions per broker and < 200,000 partitions per cluster.

components.name: Replication Factor
components.explanation: Determines how many copies of the partition exist across the cluster. A factor of 3 means 1 Leader and 2 Followers.
components.use_case: Ensuring zero data loss even if 2 brokers fail simultaneously.

components.name: In-Sync Replicas (ISR)
components.explanation: The set of follower brokers that are fully caught up with the leader. If a leader fails, only an ISR can be elected as the new leader.
components.use_case: Critical for maintaining data consistency during failover.

topics.with_key: hash(key) % numPartitions — same key always goes to same partition, ensuring ordering per key
topics.without_key: Round-robin or sticky partitioner across all partitions

example.description: Messages with the same key land in the same partition
example.code:
producer.produce('orders', key='customer-123', value='{"order_id": 1, "amount": 100}')
producer.produce('orders', key='customer-123', value='{"order_id": 2, "amount": 200}')
# Both messages go to the same partition, preserving order for customer-123

sections.name: Offsets
sections.explanation: An offset is a monotonically increasing, immutable integer assigned to each message within a partition. Offsets start at 0 and increment by 1 for each new message. Consumers use offsets to track which messages they have already processed. Offsets are stored in the internal __consumer_offsets topic. Consumers can seek to any offset — enabling replay, time-travel, and exactly-once processing.

offset_types.description: The offset of the next message to be written to the partition
The offset of the last message that has been replicated to all ISR replicas — consumers only see messages up to the HW
The last offset the consumer has acknowledged processing
The offset the consumer will fetch next

sections.name: Replication & Replication Factor
sections.explanation: Kafka replicates each partition across multiple brokers for fault tolerance. The replication factor determines how many copies of each partition exist. With replication factor 3, each partition has 1 leader and 2 followers. If the leader fails, one of the followers is elected as the new leader with no data loss.

replication_example.note: Partitions are distributed evenly so each broker is leader for roughly equal number of partitions

recommendations.min_insync_replicas: Set to replication_factor - 1 (e.g., 2 for RF=3) to prevent data loss

sections.name: Leaders & Followers (ISR)
sections.explanation: Every partition has exactly one leader broker that handles all reads and writes. Followers passively replicate the leader's log. The In-Sync Replica (ISR) set is the subset of replicas that are fully caught up with the leader (within replica.lag.time.max.ms). Only ISR members are eligible for leader election. If a follower falls behind, it is removed from the ISR.
isr_flow: Leader appends to its local log
Followers fetch from leader and replicate
Leader advances High Watermark when all ISR replicas have acknowledged
Consumer reads up to the High Watermark

sections.name: Controller Broker
sections.explanation: One broker in the cluster is elected as the Controller. The Controller is responsible for: electing partition leaders when brokers fail, managing partition state machine, handling broker joins/failures, and coordinating partition reassignments. In ZooKeeper mode, the Controller is elected via an ephemeral ZooKeeper node. In KRaft mode, the Controller role is handled by a dedicated set of controller nodes.
controller_responsibilities: Watches for broker failures via ZooKeeper (or Raft log in KRaft)
Elects new partition leaders from ISR when leader fails
Sends LeaderAndIsr requests to affected brokers
Propagates metadata changes to all brokers

sections.name: ZooKeeper vs KRaft (KIP-500)
sections.explanation: Historically, Kafka relied on Apache ZooKeeper for distributed coordination — storing broker metadata, topic configurations, consumer offsets, and controller elections. KIP-500 proposed removing this ZooKeeper dependency by implementing a built-in Raft-based metadata quorum called KRaft (Kafka Raft).

comparison.zookeeper: ZooKeeper znodes store topic metadata, broker info
comparison.kraft: Kafka internal topic __cluster_metadata stores all metadata
Raft consensus among dedicated controller nodes

comparison.zookeeper: Need to run and manage ZooKeeper cluster separately
Brokers must pull metadata — delay possible
comparison.kraft: Push-based via Raft log — faster propagation

sections.name: Log Segments & Retention
sections.explanation: Each Kafka partition is stored as a sequence of log segment files on disk. A segment is a pair of files: a .log file (actual message data) and a .index file (offset-to-byte-position index). New messages are appended to the active segment. When a segment reaches its size limit or age limit, it is rolled — a new active segment is created. Old segments are candidates for deletion based on retention policy.
segment_files: 00000000000000000000.index — sparse offset index mapping offsets to byte positions

retention_configs.log.segment.ms: 604800000 (7 days — max segment age before rolling)

sections.name: Kafka Storage Internals
sections.explanation: Kafka achieves high throughput through several storage optimizations. Sequential disk I/O — appending to log files is far faster than random writes. Page cache — Kafka leverages the OS page cache rather than maintaining its own in-memory buffer, allowing efficient data transfer between disk and network. Zero-copy — Kafka uses the Linux sendfile() syscall to transfer data directly from the page cache to the network socket without copying through user space, dramatically reducing CPU overhead and latency.

optimizations.name: Sequential I/O
optimizations.description: All writes are appends; reads are sequential fetches from offsets — disk throughput approaches RAM speed

optimizations.name: OS Page Cache
optimizations.description: Kafka writes to page cache, OS handles flushing to disk — avoids double-buffering

optimizations.name: Zero-Copy (sendfile)
optimizations.description: DMA transfer from disk to NIC buffer bypassing user space — reduces CPU by ~60-70%

optimizations.name: Batching
optimizations.description: Producers batch multiple messages; broker stores and sends in batches — amortizes overhead

optimizations.name: Compression
optimizations.description: Messages compressed at producer, stored compressed, decompressed at consumer — reduces network and storage

topics.name: Creating & Managing Topics
topics.explanation: Topics are the primary abstraction in Kafka for organizing data. They can be created manually via CLI, programmatically via AdminClient, or automatically (if auto.create.topics.enable=true). Production systems should disable auto-creation and manage topics explicitly.

key_configs.description: Max total size of partition log
Min replicas that must ack a write for it to be considered successful (used with acks=all)
Compression codec: none, gzip, snappy, lz4, zstd, producer
Size threshold for rolling a new log segment
Largest record batch the broker will accept

topics.name: Partition Count Decisions
topics.explanation: Choosing the right number of partitions is critical. Too few limits throughput and parallelism; too many increases overhead and rebalance time.
guidelines: Throughput per partition: ~10-100 MB/s depending on hardware and message size
Number of partitions >= number of consumer instances in a consumer group
Consider future consumer scaling — partitions cannot be decreased, only increased
Rule of thumb: start with 3x the number of brokers

topics.name: Compacted Topics
topics.explanation: Log compaction is an alternative to time/size-based retention. With cleanup.policy=compact, Kafka retains at least the last value for each message key. This creates a key-value store semantics where consumers always see the current state. Used extensively for Kafka Streams state stores, changelog topics, and maintaining current state of entities.
how_it_works: Log cleaner thread runs in background
Retains only the latest record for each key
Tombstone records (null value) mark key for deletion
Compacted log is always smaller than original
use_cases: User profile or entity current state

topics.name: Producer Overview
topics.explanation: A Kafka producer is a client application that publishes messages to Kafka topics. The producer handles serialization, partitioning, batching, compression, and acknowledgment. The producer is thread-safe and a single producer instance should be shared across threads for best performance.
producer_flow: 5. Sender thread flushes batches to broker based on batch.size or linger.ms
8. Callback invoked with RecordMetadata or exception

critical_configs.description: Initial brokers for cluster discovery (comma-separated host:port)
Number of retry attempts on transient failures (default: 2147483647 since 2.1)
How long to wait before sending a batch even if batch.size not reached (default: 0)
Compression: none, gzip, snappy, lz4, zstd
Total bytes of memory for buffering records (default: 33554432 = 32MB)
Max unacknowledged requests per broker (default: 5; set to 1 for strict ordering without idempotence)
Enables exactly-once delivery per producer session (default: true since Kafka 3.0)
Upper bound on total time for a record to be sent (default: 120000ms)

topics.name: Acknowledgment Modes
topics.explanation: The acks parameter controls the durability guarantee for producer writes.

modes.description: Fire and forget — producer does not wait for broker acknowledgment. Highest throughput, no durability guarantee. Messages can be lost if broker fails.
modes.use_case: Metrics collection, logs where occasional loss is acceptable

modes.description: Leader acknowledgment — leader writes to its local log and acknowledges. Moderate durability. Message can be lost if leader fails before followers replicate.
modes.use_case: Default for most use cases where some loss is tolerable

modes.description: All ISR acknowledgment — all in-sync replicas must persist the message before ack. Highest durability, lowest throughput. Must be combined with min.insync.replicas for meaningful guarantee.
modes.use_case: Financial transactions, audit logs, critical events

topics.best_practice: Use acks=all with min.insync.replicas=2 and enable.idempotence=true for strong durability without data loss.

topics.name: Retries & Idempotent Producer
topics.explanation: Retries are automatic producer retries on transient failures (network blip, leader election). However, retries can cause duplicate messages if the broker received the message but the ack was lost. The idempotent producer solves this by assigning each message a sequence number and producer ID (PID). The broker deduplicates based on PID + sequence number, ensuring exactly-once delivery per producer session.

topics.name: Producer Batching
topics.explanation: Producers accumulate messages into batches before sending to reduce network round trips and improve throughput. Two parameters control batching: batch.size (max bytes per batch) and linger.ms (how long to wait for more messages before sending).

high_throughput.description: Larger batches reduce requests but increase latency

low_latency.description: Small batches send immediately, minimize latency

topics.name: Transactional Producer
topics.explanation: Transactional producers allow atomic writes across multiple partitions and topics. Messages are either all committed or all aborted. This is the foundation for exactly-once semantics (EOS) in Kafka Streams and custom applications.

topics.name: Consumer Overview
topics.explanation: A Kafka consumer reads messages from one or more topic partitions. Consumers are organized into consumer groups for load balancing. Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within a group at any time, but a single consumer can read from multiple partitions.
consumer_flow: 1. Consumer joins a consumer group via Group Coordinator
2. Group rebalances — partitions assigned to consumers
3. Consumer sends FETCH requests to partition leaders
4. Messages returned in batch (up to max.partition.fetch.bytes)
7. On restart, consumer resumes from last committed offset

topics.name: Consumer Groups
topics.explanation: A consumer group is a set of consumers that cooperatively consume a topic. Each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. This enables horizontal scaling — add more consumers to process partitions in parallel. Multiple independent consumer groups can read the same topic independently, each with their own offset tracking.

example.description: Topic with 6 partitions consumed by a group of 3 consumers

topics.name: Offset Commit Strategies
topics.explanation: Offset commits determine at what point a consumer marks messages as 'processed'. Choosing the right strategy is critical for achieving at-least-once or exactly-once processing.

strategies.name: Auto Commit (enable.auto.commit=true)
strategies.description: Consumer automatically commits offsets every auto.commit.interval.ms. Simple but can cause duplicate processing (if consumer crashes after poll but before commit) or message loss (if commit happens before processing completes).
strategies.risk: At-least-once if consumer crashes; potential for at-most-once if enabled carelessly

strategies.name: Synchronous Manual Commit (commitSync)
strategies.description: Consumer calls commitSync() after processing. Blocks until offset is committed. Retries on failure. Simpler error handling but lower throughput.

strategies.name: Asynchronous Manual Commit (commitAsync)
strategies.description: Non-blocking commit. Does not retry on failure automatically. Should be combined with commitSync on shutdown.

strategies.name: Per-Partition Commit
strategies.description: Commit specific offsets per partition after processing each batch. Maximum control.

key_configs.description: What to do when no committed offset exists: earliest, latest, none
Max records returned per poll() call (default: 500)
Max time between poll() calls before consumer is considered dead (default: 300000ms)
Heartbeat timeout — broker removes consumer if no heartbeat within this window (default: 45000ms)
Frequency of heartbeat sends (default: 3000ms; should be 1/3 of session.timeout.ms)
Min bytes broker waits to have before responding to fetch (default: 1)
Max wait time if fetch.min.bytes not satisfied (default: 500ms)
Max bytes per partition per fetch (default: 1048576 = 1MB)

topics.name: Consumer Rebalancing
topics.explanation: A rebalance redistributes partition assignments among consumers in a group. Triggered by: consumer joining/leaving, consumer crash, topic partition count change, or subscription pattern change. During a rebalance (in eager mode), all consumers stop consuming — creating a 'stop the world' pause.

assignment_strategies.description: Assigns contiguous ranges of partitions to each consumer. Can lead to uneven distribution with multiple topics.
Round-robin distribution of all partitions across consumers. More even but causes full reassignment on changes.
Minimizes partition movements during rebalance while maintaining balance. Reduces rebalance overhead.
Incremental rebalance — only revokes partitions that must move. Consumers continue processing unaffected partitions. Eliminates stop-the-world pauses.

topics.name: Consumer Group Lifecycle

states.description: Group has no members. Offsets may still exist.
Group is gathering member join requests before assigning partitions.
Group leader has computed assignment, waiting for broker to sync.
Normal operation — partitions assigned, consumers polling.
Group removed from coordinator — all members left or expired.

join_flow: 1. Consumer sends JoinGroup request to Group Coordinator
2. First consumer to join becomes Group Leader
3. Coordinator sends member list to all members
4. Group Leader computes partition assignment using configured Assignor
5. Leader sends SyncGroup request with assignments
6. Coordinator distributes assignments to all members
7. Consumers begin fetching from assigned partitions

eager_rebalancing.description: All consumers revoke ALL their partitions at the start of rebalance. Full stop-the-world pause. Entire partition assignment is recomputed from scratch.
eager_rebalancing.drawback: Processing stops for ALL consumers during rebalance, even for partitions that don't need to move.

cooperative_rebalancing.description: Incremental rebalance in 2 rounds. Round 1: identify partitions to move. Round 2: only revoke and reassign those specific partitions. Unaffected partitions continue processing.
cooperative_rebalancing.advantage: No stop-the-world — consumers continue processing stable partitions during rebalance.

topics.name: Avoiding Rebalances
topics.explanation: Unnecessary rebalances are a major source of latency spikes in Kafka consumers. Best practices to minimize rebalances:

strategies.detail: Increase session.timeout.ms (e.g., 60000ms) to tolerate GC pauses. Keep heartbeat.interval.ms at 1/3 of session timeout.
Ensure processing time per poll() batch is less than max.poll.interval.ms. If processing is slow, reduce max.poll.records or increase max.poll.interval.ms.
Assign group.instance.id to each consumer. On restart, consumer rejoins with same identity — no rebalance triggered for up to session.timeout.ms.
Even when rebalances happen, minimize partition movements and eliminate stop-the-world behavior.

topics.name: Consumer Group Lag
topics.explanation: Consumer lag is the difference between the Log-End Offset (latest produced message) and the current committed offset of a consumer group. High lag indicates consumers are falling behind producers.

monitoring_commands.burrow: Burrow is a dedicated consumer lag monitoring tool that tracks lag trends rather than just snapshots

topics.name: What is Kafka Streams
topics.explanation: Kafka Streams is a client library (not a separate cluster) for building stateful, fault-tolerant, real-time stream processing applications on top of Apache Kafka. Unlike Flink or Spark, there is no separate processing cluster — your application IS the processing cluster. Each instance is a stream processing node. Kafka Streams uses Kafka topics as both input and output, and uses RocksDB for local state stores.
key_features: No separate cluster required — just a Java library
Elastic scaling — add/remove instances, partitions repartition automatically

comparison.represents: Infinite, unbounded stream of independent events. Each record is its own event.
comparison.example: Clickstream events, payment transactions, sensor readings
comparison.partitioning: Partitioned by key — each instance handles subset of partitions

comparison.represents: Changelog stream. Each record is an upsert — new record with same key replaces the previous value. Represents current state.
comparison.example: User profiles, account balances, inventory levels
comparison.partitioning: Partitioned — same number of partitions as input topic

comparison.represents: Like KTable but ALL partitions are replicated to EVERY instance. Enables joins without repartitioning.
comparison.analogy: Broadcast table — full dataset on every node
comparison.example: Country codes, product catalog, small reference data

code_example.description: Enriching an order stream with product data from a GlobalKTable

topics.name: Windowed Aggregations
topics.explanation: Windowing allows grouping records by time ranges for aggregation. Kafka Streams supports four window types.

window_types.description: Fixed-size, non-overlapping time windows. Each record belongs to exactly one window.
Fixed-size, overlapping windows that advance by a hop size. A record may appear in multiple windows.
window_types.example: 5-minute window that advances every 1 minute (5-minute moving average)

window_types.description: Dynamic, activity-based windows. Groups records with the same key within an inactivity gap. Window closes after inactivity gap.
Window defined by time difference between records. Contains all records within a time range of each other.
window_types.example: Events within 10 minutes of each other

topics.name: Stateful Operations & State Stores
topics.explanation: Stateful operations (aggregations, joins, windowing) require local state. Kafka Streams uses RocksDB as its default state store — an embedded key-value store optimized for SSDs. State is also backed up to Kafka changelog topics for fault tolerance.

state_store_types.description: Default. Data persisted to local disk via RocksDB. Survives application restart. Backed up to changelog topic.
state_store_types.use_case: Large state that doesn't fit in memory

state_store_types.description: State stored in memory only. Faster than RocksDB but lost on restart (rebuilt from changelog topic).

topics.fault_tolerance: If a Streams instance fails, another instance takes over its partitions and rebuilds state from the changelog topic in Kafka. State is always recoverable.

topics.name: Exactly-Once in Kafka Streams
topics.explanation: Kafka Streams supports exactly-once processing (EOS) — each input record produces exactly one output record, even in the face of failures. EOS in Kafka Streams wraps the full read-process-write cycle in a Kafka transaction.

config.processing.guarantee: exactly_once_v2 (default since Kafka 3.0; formerly exactly_once for older)
config.description: exactly_once_v2 is more efficient than the original EOS — uses one transaction per stream task rather than one per consumer group

how_it_works: Consumer reads from input partitions (within a transaction)
Output written to output topics (within same transaction)
Offsets committed atomically with output write
If failure before commit, transaction aborted — consumer resets to last committed offset

topics.name: What is ksqlDB
topics.explanation: ksqlDB is an event streaming database built on Kafka Streams that allows you to process and query Kafka data using a SQL-like language. You declare your processing logic as SQL statements and ksqlDB continuously runs them against your Kafka streams. It bridges the gap between stream processing and database querying.

components: ksqlDB Server — runs queries as Kafka Streams applications
Headless mode — run pre-defined SQL from a file

topics.name: Streams vs Tables in ksqlDB
topics.streams: Represent unbounded sequence of events. Each row is independent. Backed by a Kafka topic.
topics.tables: Represent current state — latest value per key (materialized view). Backed by a compacted Kafka topic with a state store.

examples.stream_table_join:
SELECT p.user_id, u.name, p.page
FROM pageviews p
LEFT JOIN users u ON p.user_id = u.user_id
EMIT CHANGES;
examples.windowed_aggregation:
SELECT user_id,
  COUNT(*) AS page_views,
  WINDOWSTART AS window_start,
  WINDOWEND AS window_end
FROM pageviews
WINDOW TUMBLING (SIZE 5 MINUTES)
GROUP BY user_id
EMIT CHANGES;
examples.push_query: SELECT * FROM pageviews EMIT CHANGES; -- Continuous query, results streamed to client
examples.pull_query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE user_id = 'u123'; -- Point-in-time query against materialized state

topics.name: What is Kafka Connect
topics.explanation: Kafka Connect is a scalable, fault-tolerant framework for streaming data between Apache Kafka and external systems (databases, file systems, cloud services). It eliminates the need to write custom producer/consumer code for common integrations. Connectors are reusable, configurable plugins maintained by the community and Confluent.

architecture.workers: JVM processes that run connectors and tasks. Can be standalone (single worker) or distributed (cluster of workers).
architecture.connectors: High-level configuration specifying what data to move. Spawns tasks.
architecture.tasks: Actual units of work that copy data. Parallelism is achieved by running multiple tasks per connector.
architecture.transforms: Single Message Transforms (SMTs) — lightweight, stateless record transformations applied inline.
architecture.converters: Serialize/deserialize data between Kafka's byte format and Connect's internal format (e.g., JSON, Avro).

source_connectors.description: Read from external systems and write to Kafka topics

popular.name: Debezium PostgreSQL
popular.use: CDC — streams DB changes to Kafka

popular.name: JDBC Source
popular.use: Poll database tables for new/updated rows

popular.name: AWS S3 Source
popular.use: Read files from S3 into Kafka

popular.name: MongoDB Source
popular.use: Stream MongoDB change streams to Kafka

sink_connectors.description: Read from Kafka topics and write to external systems

popular.name: S3 Sink
popular.use: Write Kafka data to S3 as Parquet/JSON/Avro files

popular.name: Snowflake Sink
popular.use: Load Kafka data into Snowflake tables

popular.name: JDBC Sink
popular.use: Write Kafka records to SQL databases

topics.name: Single Message Transforms (SMTs)
topics.explanation: SMTs are stateless, per-record transformations applied in the Kafka Connect pipeline. They can add/remove/rename fields, filter records, mask sensitive data, route records to different topics, and more.

common_smts.name: ExtractField
common_smts.description: Extract a field from a struct as the new record key or value

common_smts.name: InsertField
common_smts.description: Insert static or dynamic fields (e.g., timestamps, topic name)

common_smts.name: MaskField
common_smts.description: Replace field value with a mask (zeros, empty string) for PII/sensitive data

common_smts.name: TimestampConverter
common_smts.description: Convert timestamp formats between unix, string, Date

common_smts.name: RegexRouter
common_smts.description: Route records to different topics based on topic name regex

topics.name: What is Schema Registry & Why
topics.explanation: Schema Registry is a centralized service for managing and enforcing schemas for Kafka messages. Without schemas, Kafka producers and consumers must agree on message formats out-of-band — any change can break consumers. Schema Registry solves this by: storing schemas centrally, assigning schema IDs, and enforcing compatibility rules to prevent breaking changes.
topics.wire_format: Each Avro/Protobuf/JSON Schema message in Kafka includes a magic byte (0x00) + 4-byte schema ID + serialized payload. Consumers use the schema ID to fetch the correct schema from Schema Registry for deserialization.
benefits: Schema validation at produce time — prevents bad data from entering Kafka
Decouples producers and consumers — consumers can evolve independently
Reduces message size — schema transmitted as ID (4 bytes) not inline

modes.description: New schema can read data written with old schema. New consumers can read old messages.
safe_changes: Add a new optional field with default
modes.use_case: Most common — allows rolling upgrades of consumers before producers

modes.description: Old schema can read data written with new schema. Old consumers can still read new messages.
modes.use_case: When consumers can't be upgraded immediately

modes.description: Both BACKWARD and FORWARD compatible. Most restrictive.
safe_changes: Only adding optional fields with defaults

modes.description: No compatibility check — any schema accepted.

producer_example.description: Java producer with Avro and Schema Registry

mechanisms.description: Username/password over SASL. Simple but credentials transmitted in plaintext — MUST use SSL/TLS transport layer.
Challenge-response authentication. Passwords hashed with salt — more secure than PLAIN. Credentials stored in ZooKeeper/KRaft.
mechanisms.use_case: Recommended for username/password auth in production

mechanisms.description: Enterprise Kerberos integration. Uses service tickets for authentication. Most secure username-based auth.
mechanisms.use_case: Enterprise environments with existing Kerberos infrastructure

mechanisms.description: Both client and broker present certificates. No passwords — certificate-based identity. Each service gets its own client certificate.

topics.name: Authorization (ACLs)
topics.explanation: Kafka ACLs (Access Control Lists) control which principals (users/services) can perform which operations on which resources.

in_transit.description: Encrypt data in transit between clients and brokers, and between brokers (inter-broker replication).

at_rest.description: Kafka does not natively encrypt data at rest. Encryption at rest is typically handled by the filesystem (LUKS), cloud provider (EBS encryption, S3 SSE), or volume-level encryption.

semantics.description: Messages may be lost but never duplicated. Consumer commits offset before processing. If consumer crashes after commit but before processing, message is lost.
semantics.producer_config: acks=0 or acks=1 with no retries
semantics.trade_off: Lowest latency, highest throughput, risk of message loss

semantics.description: Messages are never lost but may be duplicated. Producer retries on failure. Consumer commits offset after successful processing. If commit fails or consumer crashes after processing but before commit, message reprocessed.
semantics.trade_off: No data loss, possible duplicates — consumer must be idempotent

semantics.description: Messages processed exactly once — no loss, no duplicates. Requires idempotent producer + Kafka transactions + atomic offset commits.
semantics.trade_off: Highest correctness guarantee, some latency overhead (~5-10%)

topics.name: Exactly-Once End-to-End
topics.explanation: End-to-end exactly-once requires coordination across producer, broker, and consumer.

components.role: Deduplicates retried messages at the broker using PID + sequence number. Exactly-once per producer session.
Atomic multi-topic writes. All messages in a transaction committed or aborted atomically.
Consumers with isolation.level=read_committed only see committed transaction messages, never aborted ones.
In Kafka Streams EOS, offsets committed as part of the same transaction as output writes.

high_throughput_config.description: Optimized for maximum throughput at cost of some latency and durability

reliable_config.description: Optimized for reliability with reasonable throughput

configs.description: Increase to reduce fetch requests and improve throughput. Set to 0 for lowest latency.
Max wait for fetch.min.bytes to be satisfied. Increase for batch-oriented processing.
Balance between processing time and throughput. Reduce if processing is slow.
Increase for large messages or high-throughput scenarios.
TCP receive buffer. Increase for high-throughput consumers.
Threads for network I/O. Increase for many client connections.
Threads for disk I/O. Increase for many partitions or slow disks.
Let OS handle flush via page cache — never force flush (performance killer).
Max bytes per fetch for replica sync. Increase if replication is bottleneck.

linux_settings.reason: Prevent kernel from swapping Kafka's page cache to disk
Increase file descriptor limit — each partition uses 2+ file handles
Allow more dirty pages before forced flush — improves write throughput
XFS preferred for Kafka log directories; avoid noatime mount option

producer_metrics.description: Failed sends per second — alert if > 0
Average produce request latency in ms
Average batch size — low value suggests suboptimal batching

consumer_metrics.description: Maximum lag across all assigned partitions — most important consumer metric

broker_metrics.description: Partitions not fully replicated — alert if > 0
Should be exactly 1 cluster-wide — alert if 0 or > 1
Partitions with no leader — critical alert
Request handler thread utilization — alert if < 0.30

topics.name: Prometheus & Grafana Setup
topics.description: Kafka exposes metrics via JMX. JMX Exporter (a Prometheus Java agent) scrapes JMX metrics and exposes them as a Prometheus endpoint.
setup_steps: 4. Add Prometheus scrape config: - job_name: kafka, static_configs: [{targets: ['broker:7071']}]
key_alerts: Consumer lag increase > threshold for > 5 minutes

tools.use_case: Move partitions between brokers for load balancing or broker decommissioning

topics.name: KRaft Architecture
topics.explanation: KRaft (Kafka Raft) replaces ZooKeeper with an internal Raft consensus protocol for metadata management. A dedicated set of controller nodes (typically 3 or 5) form a Raft quorum. All cluster metadata (topics, partitions, replicas, configs, ACLs) is stored in an internal Kafka topic called __cluster_metadata. Brokers subscribe to this topic to receive metadata updates in real time.

node_roles.description: Handles producer/consumer requests, stores partition data. Cannot participate in controller quorum.
Participates in Raft quorum for metadata management. Dedicated controller node. Does not serve client data requests.
Single node acts as both broker and controller. Suitable for development and small clusters (not recommended for large production).

topics.name: KRaft vs ZooKeeper Comparison
advantages_of_kraft: Single system to operate — no separate ZooKeeper cluster
Supports millions of partitions (ZooKeeper bottleneck removed)
Consistent metadata propagation via Raft log
Simpler security model — no separate ZooKeeper ACLs
migration_path: 2. Run kafka-storage.sh format to prepare logs for KRaft
3. Deploy controller nodes alongside existing ZooKeeper-based cluster

topics.name: Confluent Cloud
topics.description: Fully managed Kafka service by Confluent. Kafka brokers, ZooKeeper/KRaft, schema registry, and connectors fully managed. Available on AWS, GCP, and Azure.

topics.name: Docker Compose Setup
topics.description: Quick local development setup with Docker Compose

topics.name: Kafka on Kubernetes (Strimzi)
topics.description: Strimzi is a Kubernetes operator for Apache Kafka. It manages Kafka clusters as Kubernetes custom resources.

topics.name: Kafka on AWS MSK
topics.description: Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka (MSK) is a fully managed Kafka service on AWS.
features: Fully managed broker provisioning, patching, and scaling
MSK Connect — managed Kafka Connect workers

topics.name: MirrorMaker 2 (Replication)
topics.explanation: MirrorMaker 2 (MM2) is the built-in Kafka cluster replication tool, built on Kafka Connect. It replicates topics, consumer group offsets, and ACLs between Kafka clusters. Use cases: disaster recovery, data center migration, geo-distribution.

topics.name: Event Sourcing with Kafka
topics.explanation: Event sourcing is a pattern where all state changes are stored as an immutable sequence of events. Kafka's durable, append-only log is a natural fit. The current state of any entity is derived by replaying all events from the beginning.

example.description: Order lifecycle as events in Kafka
example.state_reconstruction: Replay all events with key=order-123 to get current order state

topics.name: Saga Pattern with Kafka
topics.explanation: The Saga pattern manages distributed transactions across microservices using a sequence of local transactions, each publishing events to trigger the next step. If any step fails, compensating transactions are executed.

example.scenario: Order placement across OrderService, InventoryService, PaymentService
example.compensation: If payment-failed: InventoryService releases reservation, OrderService marks order FAILED

topics.name: Outbox Pattern with Kafka
topics.explanation: Ensures atomic writes to a database AND publishing an event to Kafka. Without the outbox pattern, you risk writing to DB but failing to publish the event (or vice versa). The Outbox Pattern writes the event to an 'outbox' table in the same DB transaction, then Debezium CDC reads the outbox table and publishes to Kafka.
flow: 1. Application writes business data + event to outbox table in same transaction
2. Debezium monitors outbox table via CDC
3. Debezium publishes new rows to Kafka
5. Outbox rows marked as processed (or TTL deleted)

topics.name: Dead Letter Queue Pattern
topics.explanation: When a consumer fails to process a message after all retries, rather than blocking the main consumer or losing the message, the message is sent to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) topic. An operator can then inspect, fix, and replay messages from the DLQ.

topics.name: Kafka + Flink
topics.explanation: Apache Flink is a distributed stream processing framework with more advanced capabilities than Kafka Streams (true event-time processing, rich CEP, ML pipelines, batch-streaming unification). Kafka serves as Flink's source and sink.

flink_kafka_source.code:
KafkaSource<String> source = KafkaSource.<String>builder()
    .setBootstrapServers("localhost:9092")
    .setTopics("orders")
    .setGroupId("flink-consumer-group")
    .setStartingOffsets(OffsetsInitializer.earliest())
    .setValueOnlyDeserializer(new SimpleStringSchema())
    .build();

DataStream<String> stream = env.fromSource(source, WatermarkStrategy.noWatermarks(), "Kafka Source");

topics.name: Kafka + Spark Structured Streaming
topics.explanation: Apache Spark Structured Streaming provides micro-batch and continuous stream processing with Kafka as source/sink. Tightly integrated with Delta Lake for streaming data lakehouse patterns.

spark_kafka_read.code:
val df = spark.readStream
  .format("kafka")
  .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092")
  .option("subscribe", "orders")
  .option("startingOffsets", "earliest")
  .load()

val orders = df.selectExpr("CAST(key AS STRING)", "CAST(value AS STRING)")
  .as[(String, String)]

val query = orders.writeStream
  .format("delta")
  .outputMode("append")
  .option("checkpointLocation", "/tmp/checkpoint")
  .start("/delta/orders")

topics.name: Kafka + Debezium (CDC)
topics.explanation: Debezium is a distributed CDC (Change Data Capture) platform that captures row-level changes in databases and streams them to Kafka. It supports PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, SQL Server, Oracle, and more.
cdc_flow: 2. Each change (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) becomes a Kafka message
3. Message includes before/after snapshots of the row
5. Downstream consumers react to changes in real time

message_format.before: Row state before change (null for inserts)
message_format.after: Row state after change (null for deletes)

topics.name: Kafka + Delta Lake
topics.explanation: Delta Lake is an open-source ACID table format for data lakes. Streaming data from Kafka can be continuously written to Delta Lake tables via Spark Structured Streaming or Delta's Kafka connector. This enables streaming data lakehouse architectures.

streaming_write.code:
// Write Kafka stream to Delta Lake in real time
val query = spark.readStream
  .format("kafka")
  .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "localhost:9092")
  .option("subscribe", "orders")
  .load()
  .select(from_json(col("value").cast("string"), orderSchema).as("data"))
  .select("data.*")
  .writeStream
  .format("delta")
  .outputMode("append")
  .trigger(Trigger.ProcessingTime("1 minute"))
  .option("checkpointLocation", "/delta/_checkpoints/orders")
  .start("/delta/tables/orders")

topics.name: Log Compaction Deep Dive
topics.explanation: Log compaction retains the most recent value for each key. The log cleaner runs background threads that merge log segments, retaining only the latest value per key. Tombstones (null-value messages) mark keys for deletion — they are retained for delete.retention.ms to propagate deletes to all consumers before final removal.
compaction_flow: 1. Messages appended to active segment as normal
2. Log cleaner identifies 'dirty' segments (never compacted or modified since last compaction)
3. Cleaner builds an offset map: key -> highest offset seen
4. Cleaner iterates through dirty segments, retaining only records with highest offset per key
5. Cleaner writes cleaned segments back to disk
6. Cleaned segments merged into 'clean' portion of log

key_configs.min.cleanable.dirty.ratio: 0.5 — trigger compaction when dirty/total ratio exceeds this
key_configs.log.cleaner.min.compaction.lag.ms: 0 — min time before a message can be compacted
key_configs.log.cleaner.max.compaction.lag.ms: Long.MAX_VALUE — max time before a dirty message must be compacted

topics.name: Kafka Transactions Deep Dive
topics.explanation: Kafka transactions provide atomicity across multiple partitions and topics. The transaction protocol involves a Transaction Coordinator (a broker) and uses a two-phase commit (2PC) approach.

transaction_phases.description: Producer registers with Transaction Coordinator using transactional.id. Gets PID and epoch. Previous in-flight transactions for same transactional.id are aborted (fencing).
Client-side marker — no broker call yet.
Messages sent with transaction markers. Transaction Coordinator logs participating partitions.

1. Producer sends EndTransaction(commit) to coordinator. 2. Coordinator writes COMMIT marker to transaction log. 3. Coordinator writes COMMIT markers to all participating partitions. 4. Transaction marked complete.
Coordinator writes ABORT markers to all participating partitions. Consumers with read_committed skip aborted messages.

topics.zombie_fencing: If a producer instance crashes and restarts with the same transactional.id, the coordinator bumps the epoch. The old instance's subsequent requests are rejected — preventing zombie producers from committing stale data.

topics.name: Rack Awareness
topics.explanation: Rack awareness ensures partition replicas are spread across different physical racks, availability zones, or data centers. This way, if an entire rack or AZ fails, at least one replica of each partition is available in another rack.

config.broker_config: broker.rack=us-east-1a (set to AZ name or rack ID)
config.topic_creation: Kafka automatically distributes replicas across racks when creating topics with a rack-aware assignment strategy

topics.example: With 3 brokers in 3 AZs and replication factor 3: each partition has one replica per AZ. Losing any single AZ doesn't cause data loss or unavailability.

topics.name: Kafka Tiered Storage
topics.explanation: Tiered Storage allows Kafka to offload older log segments to cheap object storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) while keeping recent/hot data on broker local disks. Consumers transparently read from both tiers. This decouples storage capacity from broker disk size — enabling very long retention at low cost.
benefits: Unlimited retention without proportionally scaling broker disks
Broker disk savings — only recent data on fast SSD
Faster broker recovery — less local data to replicate
Cost reduction — object storage is ~10-100x cheaper than SSD

title: Top 50 Kafka Interview Questions with Answers

questions.question: What is Apache Kafka and what problems does it solve?
questions.answer: Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform that acts as a high-throughput, fault-tolerant, durable message broker and append-only log. It solves several critical problems: (1) Decoupling producers from consumers — services communicate asynchronously without knowing about each other. (2) Data loss prevention — messages are persisted to disk and replicated across brokers. (3) Scalability — horizontal scaling via partitions handles massive throughput. (4) Replay capability — unlike traditional queues, Kafka retains messages by time or size, enabling consumers to re-read historical data. (5) High throughput — sequential I/O, zero-copy, and batching enable millions of messages per second.
questions.drawbacks: Kafka adds operational complexity compared to simple message queues. ZooKeeper/KRaft management, partition tuning, and schema management require expertise.
questions.solutions: KRaft mode eliminates ZooKeeper dependency. Managed services (AWS MSK, Confluent Cloud) reduce operational burden.

questions.question: Explain the difference between a topic, partition, and offset.
questions.answer: A Topic is a logical channel or category to which producers publish and consumers subscribe — like a named stream of events (e.g., 'user-logins'). A Partition is the physical unit of parallelism — each topic is split into N partitions, each of which is an independent, ordered, append-only log stored on a broker's disk. Parallelism scales with partition count. An Offset is a monotonically increasing integer that uniquely identifies each message within a partition. Offset 0 is the first message, offset 1 the second, etc. Consumers track their position using offsets, enabling replay, exactly-once processing, and independent consumption.
questions.example: Topic 'orders' with 3 partitions: Partition 0 has offsets 0-999, Partition 1 has offsets 0-1200, Partition 2 has offsets 0-800. Consumer group A is at offset 500/700/400 respectively.
questions.drawbacks: No global ordering across partitions. Ordering is only guaranteed within a single partition.
questions.solutions: Use message keys to route related messages to the same partition (e.g., order_id as key ensures all events for an order land in the same partition).

questions.question: What is the role of the Consumer Group? What happens when a consumer joins or leaves?
questions.answer: A Consumer Group is a set of consumers that cooperatively consume a topic. Kafka ensures each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within a group at a time, enabling parallel processing. Multiple groups can independently consume the same topic with their own offsets. When a consumer joins: a rebalance is triggered — the Group Coordinator redistributes partitions among all members. When a consumer leaves (gracefully or via timeout): another rebalance redistributes the departed consumer's partitions to remaining members.
questions.drawbacks: Rebalances cause a 'stop-the-world' pause with the eager protocol — all consumers stop consuming during reassignment. Frequent rebalances (due to GC pauses, slow processing) degrade throughput.
questions.solutions: Use CooperativeStickyAssignor for incremental rebalancing. Use static group membership (group.instance.id) to prevent rebalances on restart. Tune session.timeout.ms, heartbeat.interval.ms, and max.poll.interval.ms to reduce spurious rebalances.

questions.question: Explain the three delivery semantics in Kafka: at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once.
questions.answer: At-most-once: Messages may be lost but never duplicated. Achieved by committing offsets before processing. If consumer crashes after commit but before processing, messages are lost. Use acks=0 or 1 with no retries. At-least-once: Messages are never lost but may be duplicated. Achieved by committing offsets after processing. If consumer crashes after processing but before commit, it reprocesses. Use acks=all + retries. Consumers must be idempotent. Exactly-once: Messages processed exactly once — no loss, no duplicates. Requires idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true) + transactional writes + isolation.level=read_committed on consumers. Used in Kafka Streams with processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2.
questions.drawbacks: EOS adds latency (5-10%) due to transaction coordination overhead.
questions.solutions: For most use cases, at-least-once with idempotent consumers (deduplication by message ID) is the practical choice. Reserve EOS for financial/critical use cases.

questions.question: What is the ISR (In-Sync Replica) set and why does it matter?
questions.answer: The ISR is the set of partition replicas that are fully caught up with the leader's log (within replica.lag.time.max.ms). Only ISR members can be elected as the new leader during failover. The High Watermark — the offset up to which consumers can read — only advances when all ISR replicas have replicated the message. If a replica falls behind (due to network issues, slow disk, GC pause), it's removed from the ISR. The leader continues to accept writes but with a smaller ISR.
questions.drawbacks: If ISR size falls below min.insync.replicas and acks=all, the producer gets a NotEnoughReplicasException — writes are rejected to prevent data loss. If all replicas in ISR fail, the partition goes offline.
questions.solutions: Set min.insync.replicas = RF - 1 (e.g., 2 for RF=3). Monitor UnderReplicatedPartitions metric. Set unclean.leader.election.enable=false (default in modern Kafka) to prevent data loss by not electing out-of-sync replicas.

questions.question: How does Kafka achieve high throughput? Explain the storage and networking optimizations.
questions.answer: Kafka achieves high throughput through five key optimizations: (1) Sequential I/O — all writes are appends to log files and reads are sequential — disk throughput approaches RAM speed (500MB/s+). (2) OS Page Cache — Kafka writes to the page cache rather than managing its own memory, allowing OS to efficiently flush to disk and serve reads from cache. (3) Zero-Copy (sendfile) — Linux sendfile() syscall transfers data from page cache directly to NIC buffer via DMA, bypassing user space entirely — eliminates memcpy and context switches. (4) Batching — producers accumulate messages into batches; brokers send batches to consumers — amortizes network overhead. (5) Compression — gzip/snappy/lz4/zstd compression reduces network and storage I/O.
questions.drawbacks: Zero-copy breaks down with encryption (SSL/TLS) since data must be decrypted in user space. SSL/TLS can reduce throughput by 20-30%.
questions.solutions: Use hardware SSL offloading, optimize TLS cipher suites, or use end-to-end message-level encryption for sensitive fields only rather than SSL everywhere.

questions.question: What is log compaction and when would you use it?
questions.answer: Log compaction is a retention policy (cleanup.policy=compact) where Kafka retains at least the latest value for each unique message key. The log cleaner background thread periodically scans partitions and removes older records when a newer record with the same key exists. Tombstones (null value for a key) mark keys for eventual deletion. Use log compaction for: Kafka Streams KTable changelog topics, database CDC changelogs (Debezium), entity current-state topics (user profiles, account balances), configuration topics, and any scenario where you only care about the latest state per key.
questions.drawbacks: Compaction is not instantaneous — there is always a dirty ratio before cleanup runs. Consumers reading during compaction may see 'old' records. Compaction can be CPU-intensive. No time-based guarantees — a tombstone must wait delete.retention.ms before final removal.
questions.solutions: Tune min.cleanable.dirty.ratio (lower = more frequent compaction), increase log.cleaner.threads for faster compaction, combine with delete policy (cleanup.policy=compact,delete) for both current-state and time-based retention.

questions.question: Explain KRaft mode and why Kafka is removing ZooKeeper.
questions.answer: KRaft (Kafka Raft) replaces ZooKeeper with a built-in metadata quorum using the Raft consensus algorithm. All cluster metadata is stored in an internal Kafka topic __cluster_metadata, managed by a set of controller nodes. Why ZooKeeper is being removed: (1) Operational complexity — running a separate ZooKeeper ensemble adds infrastructure overhead. (2) Scalability bottleneck — ZooKeeper tops out at ~200K partitions due to its in-memory model. KRaft supports millions of partitions. (3) Controller failover — ZooKeeper-based controller failover takes seconds; KRaft reduces this to milliseconds. (4) Security — ZooKeeper has a separate security model (ZooKeeper ACLs) vs Kafka ACLs — now unified. (5) Self-contained — Kafka becomes a single system to deploy and manage.
questions.drawbacks: Migration from ZooKeeper to KRaft requires careful planning. Some features (like JBOD support) were initially not available in KRaft. Early KRaft versions had limitations around certain admin operations.
questions.solutions: Kafka provides a migration tool (kafka-zk-migration.sh) for live migration. All features have been brought to parity in Kafka 3.7+. KRaft is production-ready and the default since Kafka 3.3.

questions.question: How does the Kafka Schema Registry work? What is the wire format?
questions.answer: The Schema Registry is a standalone REST service that stores and manages Avro/Protobuf/JSON schemas. When a producer sends a message: (1) Producer serializes the value using the schema, (2) Schema Registry assigns a schema ID (or reuses an existing one), (3) Message is prefixed with magic byte (0x00) + 4-byte schema ID + serialized payload. When a consumer receives a message: (1) Reads magic byte to know it's schema-encoded, (2) Extracts 4-byte schema ID, (3) Fetches schema from Registry (cached locally), (4) Deserializes payload using that schema. Compatibility modes (BACKWARD, FORWARD, FULL) ensure schema evolution doesn't break existing producers/consumers.
questions.drawbacks: Schema Registry becomes a critical dependency — if it goes down, producers can't register new schemas. Single point of failure in naive deployments.
questions.solutions: Deploy Schema Registry with HA (active-active mode behind a load balancer). Clients cache schemas locally — temporary Schema Registry outages don't break existing producers/consumers. AWS Glue Schema Registry is a managed alternative on AWS.

questions.question: What is the difference between KStream and KTable in Kafka Streams?
questions.answer: KStream represents an infinite, unbounded sequence of independent events — each record is a new event, independent of previous records with the same key. It's analogous to an INSERT-only database table. Example: clickstream events, payment transactions. KTable represents a changelog stream — it's a continuously updated view where each record with a key overwrites the previous value for that key. Only the latest value per key is meaningful. Analogous to a mutable database table (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). Example: current account balance, latest user profile. Operationally, KTable is backed by a local RocksDB state store and Kafka changelog topic, while KStream just processes each record as it arrives without state.
questions.drawbacks: KTable requires a state store (local disk + RocksDB) which adds resource overhead. Recovery after failure requires replaying the changelog topic, which takes time for large state.
questions.solutions: Use KTable only when you need the 'current state' semantics. For enrichment with small reference data replicated to all instances, use GlobalKTable to avoid cross-partition joins.

questions.question: What are the different types of Kafka acknowledgments and when do you use each?
questions.answer: acks=0 (fire-and-forget): Producer does not wait for broker acknowledgment. Maximum throughput but zero durability. Use for non-critical metrics, logs. acks=1 (leader acknowledgment): Leader writes to local log and acknowledges. Message can be lost if leader fails before followers replicate. Good balance of throughput and durability for most use cases. acks=all or -1 (ISR acknowledgment): All in-sync replicas must persist before ack. Maximum durability. Must pair with min.insync.replicas≥2 to be meaningful. Use for financial data, audit logs, critical events. Note: acks=all alone with min.insync.replicas=1 is equivalent to acks=1 in terms of durability.
questions.drawbacks: acks=all reduces throughput and increases latency due to waiting for all ISR replicas to respond. If a follower is slow, it affects producer latency.
questions.solutions: Tune min.insync.replicas carefully. Use compression and batching (linger.ms, batch.size) to compensate for acks=all latency. Monitor ISR shrinkage.

questions.question: How does Kafka handle message ordering guarantees?
questions.answer: Kafka guarantees strict ordering within a single partition. Messages appended to a partition maintain their insertion order. Consumers reading a partition receive messages in the exact order they were written. Across partitions, there is NO ordering guarantee. To preserve ordering for a logical entity (e.g., all events for a specific user or order), use a consistent message key — Kafka's default partitioner uses hash(key) % numPartitions, so all messages with the same key always land in the same partition.
questions.drawbacks: Multiple partitions = no global ordering. With max.in.flight.requests.per.connection > 1 and retries enabled, a retry of a failed batch could arrive out of order (batch 1 fails, batch 2 succeeds, batch 1 retried — now batch 1 is after batch 2).
questions.solutions: Enable enable.idempotence=true — this automatically sets max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5 and maintains ordering. Or set max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=1 (reduces throughput). For global ordering requirements, use a single partition topic (sacrifices scalability).

questions.question: What is the Outbox Pattern and why is it needed with Kafka?
questions.answer: The Outbox Pattern solves the dual-write problem: you need to atomically update your database AND publish an event to Kafka. Naive dual-write can fail after the DB write but before the Kafka publish (event lost) or after Kafka publish but before DB commit (phantom event). The Outbox Pattern: (1) Write business data + event to an 'outbox' table in the same database transaction. (2) Use Debezium CDC to read the outbox table and publish changes to Kafka. Now the DB transaction is the single source of truth — if it commits, the event will eventually be published.
questions.drawbacks: Adds latency (milliseconds) due to CDC pipeline. Requires Debezium infrastructure. Outbox table can grow large if not cleaned up.
questions.solutions: Use Debezium's Outbox Event Router SMT to route events from the outbox table to topic-per-aggregate-type. Periodically archive/delete processed outbox rows. Use TTL or a separate cleanup job.

questions.question: What is a compacted topic vs a regular (delete) topic?
questions.answer: A regular topic (cleanup.policy=delete) retains messages for a configured time window (retention.ms) or up to a size limit (retention.bytes). Old messages are deleted when they exceed the retention window, regardless of key. A compacted topic (cleanup.policy=compact) retains the latest value for each key forever (until a tombstone is processed). Useful when you care about current state, not history. Supports tombstones (null values) for explicit key deletion. Combined policy (cleanup.policy=compact,delete) retains latest value per key AND enforces a time-based minimum retention.
questions.drawbacks: Compaction is eventually consistent — the log cleaner runs periodically, so you may see duplicate keys in the 'dirty' portion of the log before compaction. No strict guarantee that compaction has run when you read.
questions.solutions: Consumers should handle duplicate keys gracefully (process the last value seen per key). Monitor log.cleaner.stats metrics to ensure the cleaner is keeping up.

questions.question: Explain Kafka Connect and when to use it vs writing a custom producer/consumer.
questions.answer: Kafka Connect is a framework for moving data between Kafka and external systems using pre-built, configurable connector plugins. Use Kafka Connect when: integrating with common systems (PostgreSQL via Debezium, S3, Elasticsearch, Snowflake) — these have battle-tested, maintained connectors. It handles offset management, error handling, DLQ, parallelism (tasks), and SMTs out of the box. Write a custom producer/consumer when: business logic is complex and can't be expressed with SMTs alone, the system has no available connector, performance requirements demand fine-grained control, or your data flow is highly custom.
questions.drawbacks: Kafka Connect adds operational overhead (managing Connect workers, REST API). Debugging connector issues can be complex. SMTs are stateless — complex transformations require custom code or Kafka Streams.
questions.solutions: Use Confluent Hub for battle-tested connectors. Deploy Connect in distributed mode for HA. Use the Dead Letter Queue feature for handling connector errors gracefully.

questions.question: How would you design a Kafka-based order processing system for an e-commerce platform?
questions.answer: Architecture: (1) Topics: order-created, order-validated, payment-initiated, payment-completed, inventory-reserved, order-fulfilled, order-notifications. (2) Services: OrderService publishes to order-created. Inventory/Payment/Notification services subscribe to relevant topics and publish results. (3) Partition key: order_id — ensures all events for an order are in the same partition, maintaining causal ordering. (4) Schema: Avro with Schema Registry for type safety and evolution. (5) Saga pattern for distributed transactions — OrderOrchestrator tracks state machine, handles compensating transactions on failure. (6) Exactly-once: enable.idempotence=true + acks=all for payment events. At-least-once with idempotent consumers for notifications. (7) Monitoring: lag alerts per consumer group, DLQ for failed events.
questions.drawbacks: Eventual consistency — downstream services process events asynchronously, so order state may be temporarily inconsistent across services. Saga failures require complex compensating logic.
questions.solutions: Use CQRS — separate read models per service that consume events and maintain local read-optimized state. Implement idempotent event handlers. Use correlation IDs for distributed tracing.

questions.question: How do you decide on the number of partitions for a topic?
questions.answer: Partition count determines max parallelism for both producers and consumers. Formula: partitions = max(desired_throughput / throughput_per_partition). Throughput per partition: ~10-100 MB/s depending on broker hardware, compression, and message size. Consumer parallelism: partitions >= max consumers you'll ever need in the group. Start with 3-6x broker count for balanced distribution. Rules: (1) More partitions = more parallelism but higher overhead (leader election time, file handles, memory for replication). (2) Partitions cannot be decreased — only increased. (3) Too many partitions slows controller failover and increases end-to-end latency in ZooKeeper mode. (4) For low-latency topics, prefer fewer partitions to reduce fanout.
questions.drawbacks: You can't reduce partitions later. Over-partitioning increases recovery time, leader election time, and broker memory usage. Under-partitioning limits throughput and consumer parallelism.
questions.solutions: Start conservatively (e.g., 2x brokers) and increase later via kafka-topics.sh --alter. Plan for consumer scaling from the start — you can always add consumers up to partition count.

questions.question: How do you implement exactly-once processing in Kafka end-to-end?
questions.answer: End-to-end exactly-once requires three components: (1) Idempotent Producer (enable.idempotence=true): Broker deduplicates retried messages using PID + sequence number. Ensures exactly-once delivery per session. (2) Transactional Producer: Use beginTransaction()/commitTransaction() to atomically write to multiple partitions/topics. (3) Transactional Consumer: Set isolation.level=read_committed — consumer only reads committed transaction messages, skips aborted ones. (4) Atomic Offset Commit: Use producer.sendOffsetsToTransaction() to commit consumer offsets inside the producer transaction — makes the consume-transform-produce cycle atomic. In Kafka Streams, set processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2 to get this automatically.
questions.drawbacks: EOS adds ~5-10% latency overhead. Transaction coordinator can become a bottleneck under high load. Zombie fencing requires unique transactional.id per producer instance.
questions.solutions: Use exactly_once_v2 (not exactly_once) which is more efficient — one transaction per task. Make transactional.id unique and stable across restarts (e.g., combine app name + partition ID).

questions.question: Describe the Lambda Architecture with Kafka.
questions.answer: Lambda Architecture is a big data pattern that combines batch and streaming processing to handle massive data sets. Kafka layer: serves as the backbone for both layers. Batch Layer (Cold Path): Raw data stored in immutable append-only data lake (HDFS/S3). Periodic batch jobs (Spark) recompute accurate views over historical data. Stores complete truth. Speed Layer (Hot Path): Kafka + Spark Streaming or Kafka Streams processes recent data in real time. Generates approximate (low-latency) views for recent data. Serving Layer: Merges batch views (high accuracy) with speed layer views (recency) for queries.
questions.drawbacks: Complexity of maintaining two separate processing codebases (batch and streaming) that must produce the same results. Synchronizing results at query time is complex. High operational overhead.
questions.solutions: Kappa Architecture simplifies this by eliminating the batch layer — only a streaming layer, using Kafka as the replayable source of truth. Replay historical events through the stream processor when a full recompute is needed. Delta Lake / Apache Iceberg enable 'streaming-first' lakehouse patterns that further blur the batch/streaming boundary.

questions.question: How would you design a multi-region Kafka setup for disaster recovery?
questions.answer: Options: (1) MirrorMaker 2 (MM2): Active-Passive replication. MM2 continuously replicates topics from primary cluster to DR cluster. Prefixes replicated topics (e.g., us-east.orders). Offsets translated via __consumer_offsets.checkpoint topics. Failover: update consumer bootstrap.servers to DR cluster. (2) Confluent Cluster Linking: Lower-latency replication (mirrors log segments directly). Provides automatic offset translation. Easier failover. (3) Stretch Cluster (multi-region single cluster): Brokers spread across regions. min.insync.replicas requires acks from both regions. Higher latency writes but zero-RPO. Only feasible for low-latency region pairs.
questions.drawbacks: MM2 has eventual consistency — some messages may not be replicated (RPO > 0). Failover requires consumer reconfiguration. Offset translation is not perfect.
questions.solutions: Use MM2 with sync.group.offsets.enabled=true and emit.checkpoints.interval.seconds=30 to keep consumer offsets synchronized. Test failover procedures regularly. For RPO=0, use synchronous replication (stretch cluster) at the cost of higher write latency.

questions.question: How would you implement a CDC (Change Data Capture) pipeline with Kafka?
questions.answer: CDC Pipeline with Debezium: (1) Enable replication/WAL on source DB (e.g., PostgreSQL: wal_level=logical, create replication slot and publication). (2) Deploy Debezium Kafka Connect connector pointed at source DB. (3) Debezium reads binlogs/WAL and publishes change events to Kafka topics (one topic per table: dbserver.schema.table). (4) Change events include before/after row images, operation type (c/u/d/r), and transaction metadata. (5) Downstream consumers process change events: transform and load into data lake (S3/Delta), replicate to read replicas, invalidate caches, trigger business events. (6) Use Kafka Connect S3 Sink for data lake ingestion, JDBC Sink for database replication.
questions.drawbacks: Debezium initial snapshot for large tables can take hours and creates high load on source DB. Schema changes in source DB require connector restart or careful evolution. Replication slot lag in PostgreSQL can cause WAL disk consumption.
questions.solutions: Use snapshot.mode=schema_only for non-initial deployments. Monitor PostgreSQL replication slot lag. Use pg_replication_slot_lag alert. For large tables, use chunk-based initial snapshotting (snapshot.max.threads).

questions.question: When would you use Kafka Streams vs Flink vs Spark Structured Streaming?
questions.answer: Kafka Streams: Use when processing logic is relatively simple to moderate, you want no separate cluster, you're already a Java/JVM shop, and the application is closely tied to Kafka. Best for stateful per-entity aggregations, table joins, and enrichments. Flink: Use for complex event processing (CEP), true event-time processing with late data handling, ML model serving, very large state, or when you need sub-second latency with exactly-once. Flink has the richest windowing and watermark semantics. Spark Structured Streaming: Use when you're already in the Spark/Databricks ecosystem, need tight integration with Delta Lake, or have team Spark expertise. Micro-batch model (default) adds 50-500ms latency. Continuous mode available for lower latency.

comparison_matrix.cluster_required: Flink: Yes, Spark: Yes, Kafka Streams: No

questions.question: How do you handle schema evolution in Kafka topics?
questions.answer: Schema evolution allows changing message structure without breaking existing producers or consumers. Strategy: (1) Use Schema Registry with Avro/Protobuf/JSON Schema. (2) Choose compatibility mode: BACKWARD (new schema reads old data — consumers upgrade first), FORWARD (old schema reads new data — producers upgrade first), FULL (both). (3) Safe changes: add optional fields with defaults, remove optional fields. Unsafe: rename fields, change field types, remove required fields.
