(4) For major breaking changes: create a new topic version (orders-v2) and dual-publish during migration period. (5) Use SpecificRecord (code generation) or GenericRecord (dynamic) in Avro.

example.breaking_change_handling: For breaking changes: create new topic, dual-write to both topics during transition, migrate consumers, deprecate old topic

questions.drawbacks: Schema Registry compatibility mode is topic-scoped, not global. Different subjects can have different compatibility modes.
questions.solutions: Set a default global compatibility mode. Use CI/CD pipeline that validates schema compatibility before deployment (mvn io.confluent:kafka-schema-registry-maven-plugin:test-compatibility).

questions.question: Explain the CQRS pattern with Kafka.
questions.answer: CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) separates write operations (commands) from read operations (queries). With Kafka: (1) Write side: Commands go to a service that validates and publishes events to Kafka. The service's source of truth is the event log. (2) Read side: Multiple projections subscribe to Kafka events and maintain read-optimized materialized views in different stores (Elasticsearch for search, Redis for caching, PostgreSQL for complex queries). (3) Each projection can be rebuilt by replaying events from the beginning. Benefits: each read model is optimized for its query pattern. Write and read paths scale independently.
questions.drawbacks: Eventual consistency between write and read models — a read immediately after a write may not reflect the latest state. Increased complexity — multiple read models to maintain.
questions.solutions: Accept eventual consistency as a feature, not a bug. Use event timestamps for version checks. Provide 'consistency tokens' to clients for read-after-write consistency. Use Kafka Streams or ksqlDB to maintain projections.

questions.question: How would you implement a real-time fraud detection system using Kafka?
questions.answer: Architecture: (1) Ingest: Payment transactions published to 'transactions' topic (partitioned by customer_id for per-customer ordering). (2) Real-time scoring: Kafka Streams application reads transactions, maintains sliding window of recent transactions per customer (state store), computes features (tx frequency, amount deviation, geo-distance from last tx). (3) Model serving: Enriched features sent to ML model (inline ONNX model or gRPC to model server). (4) Alerting: High-risk transactions published to 'fraud-alerts' topic. Low confidence scores go to 'review-queue'. (5) Feedback loop: Human review decisions published back to Kafka, consumed by ML training pipeline. (6) Pattern-based rules: ksqlDB for rule-based detection (COUNT transactions in window > threshold).
questions.drawbacks: Feature computation latency adds to transaction processing time. Model staleness — deployed model may not reflect latest fraud patterns.
questions.solutions: Use async fraud scoring (return decision quickly, flag for review if delayed). Implement model hot-swap without restart. Use A/B testing by partitioning traffic between model versions.

questions.question: What are the key configurations for maximizing Kafka producer throughput?
questions.answer: For maximum throughput: batch.size=131072 (128KB) — larger batches reduce requests per second. linger.ms=20 — wait up to 20ms to accumulate a full batch. compression.type=lz4 — fast compression with good ratio (snappy/zstd for better compression). buffer.memory=67108864 — 64MB buffer prevents back pressure. acks=1 — only wait for leader ack. max.in.flight.requests.per.connection=5 — allow 5 outstanding requests. Avoid: linger.ms=0 (sends every record immediately), compression=none on high-volume topics, acks=all without compensation elsewhere.
questions.benchmark_approach: Use kafka-producer-perf-test.sh: kafka-producer-perf-test.sh --topic test --num-records 10000000 --record-size 1024 --throughput -1 --producer-props bootstrap.servers=localhost:9092 batch.size=131072 linger.ms=20
questions.drawbacks: Higher linger.ms increases end-to-end latency. Larger batches use more memory. Compression adds CPU overhead.
questions.solutions: Profile CPU vs throughput tradeoff. lz4 is CPU-efficient. snappy is slightly slower but better compression. zstd offers best compression ratio at moderate CPU cost. Choose based on your bottleneck (network vs CPU).

questions.question: How do you tune Kafka consumers for high throughput?
questions.answer: Key consumer tuning parameters: (1) fetch.min.bytes=65536 — broker waits until 64KB ready before responding (reduces fetch round trips). (2) fetch.max.wait.ms=500 — max wait time for fetch.min.bytes condition. (3) max.poll.records=1000 — process more records per poll call. (4) max.partition.fetch.bytes=10485760 — 10MB per partition per fetch. (5) Add consumers up to partition count for parallel processing. (6) Process records asynchronously — use thread pool per consumer for I/O-bound processing. (7) Batch commit offsets rather than committing per record.
questions.multi_threaded_pattern: One consumer thread per partition, or use a single consumer + worker thread pool with manual offset management and careful offset tracking.
questions.drawbacks: Higher max.poll.records can cause max.poll.interval.ms violation if processing is slow. Large fetch.min.bytes adds latency to low-throughput topics.
questions.solutions: For slow processing: reduce max.poll.records or increase max.poll.interval.ms. For high-throughput: use async processing but track offsets carefully to avoid reprocessing or loss.

questions.question: How does compression work in Kafka and which algorithm should you use?
questions.answer: Producers compress message batches before sending. The compressed batch is stored as-is on the broker (no decompression at broker). Consumers decompress upon receipt. Compression ratios and CPU costs: gzip (5:1 ratio, high CPU — use for large messages where bandwidth is the bottleneck), snappy (2-3:1 ratio, moderate CPU — good default), lz4 (2-3:1 ratio, lowest CPU, fastest — best for high-throughput general use), zstd (4:1+ ratio, moderate CPU — best compression/speed tradeoff, recommended for new deployments). Set at topic level (compression.type=lz4) to override producer setting.
questions.drawbacks: Compression breaks zero-copy optimization on some older Kafka versions (broker must decompress to apply SMTs or verify message format). CPU usage increases on producers and consumers.
questions.solutions: Monitor producer CPU and compression ratio metrics. Use lz4 as default — it's fastest and has good enough ratio. Use zstd for topics with large JSON/text payloads where compression ratio matters.

questions.question: How do you optimize broker configuration for high throughput?
questions.answer: Key broker settings: (1) num.network.threads = 2 * CPU_CORES — handle client connections. (2) num.io.threads = 2 * CPU_CORES — handle disk I/O. (3) socket.send.buffer.bytes=1048576, socket.receive.buffer.bytes=1048576 — TCP buffers. (4) log.flush.interval.messages=Long.MAX_VALUE — never force flush (let OS page cache handle it). (5) replica.fetch.max.bytes=10485760 — allow larger replication fetch. (6) Use multiple JBOD disk paths (log.dirs=/disk1/kafka,/disk2/kafka) for parallel disk I/O. OS-level: vm.swappiness=1, increase nofile limit, use XFS filesystem.
questions.drawbacks: Too many threads causes context switching overhead. Disabling log flush (log.flush.interval.messages=MAX_VALUE) means data in page cache can be lost on power failure.
questions.solutions: Enable replication (RF≥2) — replication provides durability without manual flush. Use cloud volumes (EBS gp3) with consistent performance. Monitor RequestHandlerAvgIdlePercent — below 30% means add more threads.

questions.question: What is consumer lag and how do you minimize it?
questions.answer: Consumer lag is log_end_offset - committed_consumer_offset for each partition. It represents how far behind consumers are from producers. To minimize lag: (1) Scale consumers to match partition count. (2) Optimize processing logic — profile the consumer code, use async I/O for external calls. (3) Increase max.poll.records and tune processing parallelism. (4) Increase fetch sizes (fetch.min.bytes, max.partition.fetch.bytes). (5) Use more partitions to allow more consumer parallelism. (6) Fix slow downstream systems (DB writes, API calls) that block consumer processing. (7) Enable consumer-side parallelism for I/O-bound tasks.
questions.monitoring: Use kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe or Burrow (lag trend analysis) or Prometheus kafka_consumer_group_lag metric.
questions.drawbacks: High lag can indicate a data surge or consumer bug. Growing lag can lead to retention window expiry — messages deleted before consumption.
questions.solutions: Set consumer group lag alerts. If lag consistently grows, it means throughput is insufficient — add consumers and/or partitions. Increase retention as a safety buffer.

questions.question: How do you benchmark Kafka throughput?
questions.answer: Kafka ships with built-in performance test tools: Producer: kafka-producer-perf-test.sh --topic perf-test --num-records 10000000 --record-size 1024 --throughput -1 --producer-props bootstrap.servers=localhost:9092 batch.size=131072 linger.ms=5 acks=1. Consumer: kafka-consumer-perf-test.sh --topic perf-test --messages 10000000 --bootstrap-server localhost:9092. End-to-end latency: kafka-producer-perf-test.sh with --print-metrics flag. Key metrics to observe: records/sec, MB/sec, 50th/95th/99th percentile latency, and CPU/disk utilization.

benchmark_targets.high_throughput: > 1 million records/sec / broker on modern hardware
benchmark_targets.disk_throughput: 500-1000 MB/s with sequential writes on SSD

questions.drawbacks: Synthetic benchmarks don't capture real workload patterns (variable message size, bursty traffic, mixed read/write).
questions.solutions: Benchmark with representative message sizes and patterns. Test under realistic load. Monitor GC pauses (G1GC recommended for brokers).

questions.question: How does Kafka's zero-copy mechanism work and when does it break down?
questions.answer: Zero-copy uses Linux's sendfile() or transferTo() syscall to copy data from the OS page cache (where Kafka's log files are cached) directly to the NIC buffer via DMA, without involving user space or a CPU memcpy. This eliminates: kernel buffer → user buffer copy, user buffer → socket buffer copy, two context switches. Result: ~4x throughput improvement for fetch operations, significantly reduced CPU usage. When zero-copy breaks down: SSL/TLS is enabled (data must be decrypted in user space — memcpy required), message format conversion needed (compression.type mismatch between producer, broker, and consumer), SMTs applied at broker level.
questions.drawbacks: SSL/TLS eliminates zero-copy benefit — encrypted Kafka has notably higher CPU usage than plaintext.
questions.solutions: For SSL overhead: use faster cipher suites (AES-NI hardware acceleration), increase num.network.threads to compensate, or selectively encrypt only sensitive topics. For message format: set broker compression.type=producer to preserve producer's compression and avoid recompression.

questions.question: What JVM settings should you tune for Kafka brokers?
questions.answer: Key JVM settings for Kafka brokers: Heap: KAFKA_HEAP_OPTS="-Xms6g -Xmx6g" — 6GB is a good starting point. Set Xms=Xmx to prevent heap resizing. Don't allocate too much heap — leave RAM for OS page cache (which Kafka relies on heavily). GC: -XX:+UseG1GC (default in modern JVMs) — good for large heaps. -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=20 — target max GC pause. -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=16m — match to typical object sizes. -XX:+ExplicitGCInvokesConcurrent to prevent stop-the-world System.gc(). JMX: -Dcom.sun.management.jmxremote for metrics exposure.
questions.drawbacks: Too much heap reduces page cache available for Kafka log data — degrades read performance. GC pauses cause ISR shrinkage (follower lag) and consumer rebalances.
questions.solutions: Monitor GC pause duration. Keep heap ≤ 8GB. For high-memory machines (64GB+), give 50-60% to OS page cache and keep heap at 6-8GB.

questions.question: How does partition count affect Kafka end-to-end latency?
questions.answer: More partitions generally increase end-to-end latency in Kafka: (1) Producer-side: with sticky partitioner, messages for different partitions may not be batched together — more requests for the same data volume. (2) Broker-side: more partitions = more file handles, more leader election candidates, more metadata to manage. (3) Consumer-side: more partitions = more fetch requests, more state to track. (4) Controller failover: more partitions means longer controller failover time (must send LeaderAndIsr requests to all affected brokers). In ZooKeeper mode, each partition requires ZooKeeper znodes, adding overhead.
questions.numbers: LinkedIn found that end-to-end latency roughly doubled for every 6x increase in partition count beyond their baseline.
questions.drawbacks: Many small topics each with many partitions compound the problem. 50 topics x 100 partitions = 5000 partitions — significant overhead.
questions.solutions: In KRaft mode, partition count scalability is much better (1M+ partitions tested). Avoid over-partitioning for low-throughput topics. Use fewer partitions for latency-sensitive topics.

questions.question: Explain the Sticky Partitioner and why it was introduced.
questions.answer: The original default partitioner (round-robin without key) sent each message to a different partition, preventing effective batching — every message was its own single-record batch. The Sticky Partitioner (introduced in Kafka 2.4) assigns all keyless messages to one 'sticky' partition until the batch is full (batch.size reached) or the linger.ms timer fires. Then it randomly picks a new sticky partition. This allows messages to accumulate into larger batches before sending, reducing the number of requests and improving throughput by 50-100% for keyless producers. CooperativeSticky is the cooperative rebalancing version for consumers.
questions.drawbacks: Messages going to the same partition may create a temporary hotspot if partition assignment is uneven or if one partition's broker is slower.
questions.solutions: Sticky partitioner rebalances stickiness randomly, so hotspots are transient. Monitor per-partition throughput metrics. If persistent skew, use custom partitioner.

questions.question: Consumer lag is growing continuously despite consumers being up. How do you diagnose and fix this?
questions.answer: Diagnosis steps: (1) Check consumer group status: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe — look at LAG column and CONSUMER-ID to confirm consumers are assigned. (2) Check consumer processing time — if processing takes longer than messages arrive, lag will grow. Profile the consumer code. (3) Check partition count vs consumer count — if consumers < partitions, some partitions may be unassigned. Add consumers. (4) Check for consumer crashes/rebalances — frequent rebalances waste time on reassignment. (5) Check downstream bottlenecks — slow DB writes, external API calls. (6) Check if any consumer thread is blocked (thread dump). (7) Check for message processing errors being swallowed. Fixes: add more consumers (up to partition count), optimize processing code, increase max.poll.records + async processing, reduce downstream latency, add partitions + consumers.
questions.drawbacks: Adding consumers beyond partition count doesn't help (excess consumers are idle).
questions.solutions: First add consumers up to partition count. If still lagging, increase partitions (requires partition reassignment). Consider parallelizing processing within each consumer using a thread pool.

questions.question: Producers are getting NotEnoughReplicasException. What's happening and how do you fix it?
questions.answer: NotEnoughReplicasException (or NotEnoughReplicasAfterAppendException) is thrown when the number of in-sync replicas (ISR) falls below min.insync.replicas while acks=all. Root causes: (1) Broker(s) down or unreachable — ISR shrinks. (2) Followers falling behind due to slow disk/network — removed from ISR. (3) min.insync.replicas set too high relative to available ISR. Diagnosis: Check kafka.server:type=ReplicaManager,name=UnderReplicatedPartitions metric. Check if brokers are all up. Check replica.lag.time.max.ms — increase if followers are slow. Check follower disk I/O and network metrics. Fix: Restore failed brokers. If brokers are up but followers are lagging, investigate disk/network bottleneck. Temporarily reduce min.insync.replicas (not recommended long-term).
questions.drawbacks: Reducing min.insync.replicas to 1 effectively negates the durability benefit of replication.
questions.solutions: In production: RF=3, min.insync.replicas=2. This tolerates 1 broker failure while maintaining write availability. Monitor UnderReplicatedPartitions and alert immediately.

questions.question: Consumer is throwing CommitFailedException or max.poll.interval.ms exceeded. How do you diagnose?
questions.answer: CommitFailedException with message 'Commit cannot be completed since the group has already rebalanced and assigned the partitions to another member' is caused by: (1) Consumer took longer than max.poll.interval.ms to process a batch and call poll() again — broker assumed it died and rebalanced. (2) Consumer was doing heavy processing between poll() calls. Fixes: (1) Reduce max.poll.records to process fewer records per poll. (2) Increase max.poll.interval.ms to give more processing time. (3) Move slow processing to async — use thread pool, call poll() from main thread frequently, commit offsets manually from processing thread when complete. (4) Profile and optimize processing logic. (5) Reduce downstream I/O in the hot path.
questions.drawbacks: Increasing max.poll.interval.ms delays detection of truly dead consumers.
questions.solutions: Use consumer.pause() / resume() for flow control. Consider consumer.assign() + manual partition management for fine-grained control.

questions.question: Kafka broker is running out of disk space. What are your options?
questions.answer: Immediate options: (1) Delete data from topics with long retention: kafka-configs.sh --alter --add-config retention.ms=3600000 --entity-type topics --entity-name big-topic (temporarily reduce retention to trigger deletion). (2) Delete unused/test topics: kafka-topics.sh --delete. (3) Add disk capacity — attach additional volumes and add to log.dirs (JBOD). Long-term options: (4) Tune retention policies per topic based on consumer needs. (5) Enable compression for high-volume topics. (6) Enable Tiered Storage (offload cold segments to S3/GCS). (7) Add brokers and reassign partitions for load balancing. (8) Use kafka-log-dirs.sh to identify which topics/partitions use the most space.
questions.drawbacks: Reducing retention mid-stream can cause consumers to miss messages if they're behind.
questions.solutions: Always monitor disk utilization with alerts at 70% and 85% usage. Plan retention policies proactively. Enable Tiered Storage for long-retention topics from the start.

questions.question: How do you debug a consumer that is reading duplicate messages?
questions.answer: Duplicate messages in Kafka are usually caused by: (1) Consumer crash after processing but before offset commit — most common cause. (2) Rebalance during processing — consumer was processing, rebalance happened, partition reassigned, new consumer starts from last committed offset. (3) Large max.poll.interval.ms combined with slow processing. (4) enable.auto.commit=true with insufficient commit interval — processing between commits can be replayed. Diagnosis: Add unique message ID to each event. Log message ID + processing timestamp. Check for duplicate IDs in output. Fixes: (1) Make consumers idempotent (upsert instead of insert, deduplication by ID). (2) Use manual offset commit (enable.auto.commit=false) and commit only after successful processing + output persistence. (3) Use Kafka Streams or transactions for exactly-once. (4) Store processed message IDs in Redis/DB with TTL for deduplication.
questions.drawbacks: Full exactly-once is complex and adds latency. Idempotent consumers require unique IDs in every message.
questions.solutions: Add a UUID or business key (order_id, event_id) to all messages. Make all downstream operations idempotent. This is more practical than full EOS for most use cases.

questions.question: A Kafka Connect connector keeps failing and restarting. How do you debug?
questions.answer: Debugging steps: (1) Check connector status via REST API: GET /connectors/{name}/status. (2) Check connector task logs for error messages. (3) Check Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) topic for failed records (if configured). (4) Verify connectivity to source/sink system (can Connect workers reach the DB/S3/etc.?). (5) Validate connector configuration — common issues: wrong credentials, incorrect JDBC driver, schema mismatch. (6) Check Schema Registry connectivity for Avro converters. (7) For Debezium: check DB replication slot status, WAL level. Common failures: ClassNotFoundException (missing connector JAR in plugin.path), SerializationException (schema not registered), ConnectException (DB connection refused), DataException (type conversion failure). Fix: correct config, install missing JARs, configure DLQ for error isolation.
questions.drawbacks: DLQ masks errors — bad records silently go to DLQ without alerting. Connector restart loops waste resources.
questions.solutions: Monitor connector status via Prometheus. Alert on connector status != RUNNING. Configure DLQ + log errors. Set errors.retry.timeout appropriately.

questions.question: How do you handle a Kafka broker failure in production?
questions.answer: Immediate response: (1) Kafka's controller automatically detects broker failure via ZooKeeper/KRaft and triggers leader election for affected partitions. With RF=3 and min.insync.replicas=2, producers/consumers continue without manual intervention. (2) Monitor: UnderReplicatedPartitions will spike — expected during recovery. (3) Check consumer lag — new leaders must catch up on replication queue. Recovery: (4) If broker data is intact: restart the broker. It will catch up replication automatically. (5) If broker disk failed: provision new broker with same broker.id (or new ID with partition reassignment). (6) Wait for ISR to recover (UnderReplicatedPartitions returns to 0). (7) Run preferred leader election to rebalance: kafka-leader-election.sh --election-type PREFERRED --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --all-topic-partitions.
questions.drawbacks: Recovery time depends on how much data the new/restarted broker must replicate. Large partitions can take hours to recover.
questions.solutions: Use rack awareness to spread replicas across AZs. Keep log.dirs on fast SSDs to speed recovery. Monitor under-replicated partitions and trigger alerts for SLA tracking.

questions.question: Messages are being produced to Kafka but consumers aren't receiving them. What could be wrong?
questions.answer: Checklist: (1) Topic subscription: is the consumer subscribed to the correct topic? Check consumer.subscribe() vs consumer.assign(). (2) Offset reset: new consumer group with auto.offset.reset=latest won't see messages produced before it started. Change to earliest or use --reset-offsets. (3) Consumer group assignment: run kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe — are all partitions assigned? (4) Partition assignment: consumer count > partition count means some consumers get no partitions. (5) Filter/deserialization error: consumer may be throwing exceptions silently and not processing. Enable error logging. (6) Message format: if producer uses Avro but consumer expects plain JSON, deserialization fails. (7) Security: consumer may lack ACL to READ the topic. Check Kafka ACLs. (8) Network: consumer can't reach broker — check firewall, VPC, security groups.
diagnostic_commands: kafka-consumer-groups.sh --describe --group my-group — check partition assignment and offsets
kafka-console-consumer.sh --from-beginning — verify messages are actually in the topic
kafka-topics.sh --describe — verify topic exists and partition count

questions.question: Explain how Kafka's idempotent producer works internally.
questions.answer: When enable.idempotence=true, the broker assigns each producer a unique Producer ID (PID) and an epoch (incremented on each initTransactions() call or producer restart). The producer maintains a per-partition sequence number, starting at 0 and incrementing for each message. Every ProduceBatch includes {PID, Epoch, PartitionID, SequenceNumber}. The broker maintains an in-memory map of {PID, PartitionID} -> last 5 sequence numbers. On receiving a batch: if SeqNum == expected: accept and advance. If SeqNum < expected (duplicate): reject with DuplicateSequenceNumber (no error to producer — silently deduplicated). If SeqNum > expected (gap): reject with OutOfOrderSequenceNumber (producer error). The idempotent producer guarantees exactly-once per-producer-session per-partition — not across sessions (PID changes on restart unless transactional.id is set).
questions.drawbacks: PID is per-session — on restart without transactional.id, a new PID is assigned and sequence numbers reset. This means deduplication only works within a single producer session.
questions.solutions: Use transactional.id for cross-session idempotence. transactional.id is stable across restarts, the epoch is incremented to fence previous instances.

questions.question: How does the Kafka Raft (KRaft) consensus protocol work?
questions.answer: KRaft implements a variant of the Raft consensus protocol for metadata management. Three to five nodes form the controller quorum. One node is elected the Active Controller (leader in Raft terms). The others are follower/observer controllers. The Active Controller processes all metadata changes (new topics, partition reassignments, broker changes) and writes them as records to the __cluster_metadata topic (the Raft log). All metadata is stored as event records in this log — much like Kafka itself is an event log! Controller election: uses Raft leader election — a candidate requests votes, wins if it gets majority. Epoch-based fencing prevents split-brain. Brokers subscribe to the __cluster_metadata topic as observers — they receive all metadata changes in real time. No separate fetch for metadata — it's push-based via the Raft log subscription.
questions.drawbacks: In early KRaft versions, combined broker+controller mode is not recommended for large clusters. Dedicated controller quorum is preferred.
questions.solutions: For production: run 3 dedicated controller nodes + N broker nodes. This isolates controller quorum from broker churn.

questions.question: What is the Kafka consumer protocol and how does the new KIP-848 protocol differ?
questions.answer: Classic Consumer Protocol (current): Heartbeat-based. Consumers send heartbeats to the Group Coordinator to stay alive. Offset management and group membership are handled by the same coordinator. Rebalances are triggered by JoinGroup/SyncGroup requests — all members participate even if only one partition moves. Has session.timeout.ms-based failure detection. KIP-848 Next-Gen Protocol (preview): Decouples membership from partition assignment. Introduces server-side assignment — the broker computes partition assignments, not the group leader. No more group leader consumer — the coordinator is authoritative. Incremental reconciliation — only partitions that need to change are affected. Faster rebalance triggering — the server detects changes immediately, not on next heartbeat cycle. Stateless consumers — consumers don't need to run a rebalance protocol themselves.
questions.drawbacks: KIP-848 is still in preview (as of Kafka 3.7-3.8). Not yet recommended for production.
questions.solutions: Use CooperativeStickyAssignor in the classic protocol as the current best option for minimal rebalance impact.

questions.question: Explain Kafka's transaction protocol and how zombie fencing works.
questions.answer: Kafka transactions use a two-phase commit (2PC) protocol: Phase 1 (Prepare): Producer calls beginTransaction(). On first produce, the Transaction Coordinator (specific broker) records participating partitions in the transaction log (__transaction_state topic). Phase 2 (Commit): Producer calls commitTransaction(). Coordinator writes COMMIT to __transaction_state. Then writes COMMIT markers to all participating partitions. Consumers with read_committed skip any records between BEGIN and ABORT markers. Zombie Fencing: If a producer crashes and a new instance starts with the same transactional.id, the coordinator increments the producer epoch. The old (zombie) instance's subsequent requests are rejected with ProducerFencedException because their epoch is stale. This prevents two instances from simultaneously committing transactions — critical for exactly-once guarantees.
questions.example: transactional.id = 'payment-processor-partition-5' — unique per consumer partition being processed. On restart, old epoch is fenced, new instance takes over atomically.
questions.drawbacks: transactional.id must be stable and unique per logical stream. Loss of transactional.id mapping causes issues.
questions.solutions: Derive transactional.id from the input partition being consumed: '{app-name}-{topic}-{partition}'. This ensures one producer instance per input partition and proper fencing.

questions.question: How does Kafka Streams handle fault tolerance and state store recovery?
questions.answer: Kafka Streams fault tolerance has two layers: (1) Task failover: If a Streams instance fails, another instance takes over its stream tasks (input partitions). Since tasks are stateless at the Kafka level (state is backed by local RocksDB + changelog topic), any instance can take over any task. (2) State recovery: The RocksDB local store is backed by a Kafka changelog topic (one partition per state store partition). On takeover, the new instance replays the changelog topic to rebuild the RocksDB state. Standby replicas: Configure num.standby.replicas > 0 to have standby instances pre-populate state stores from changelog topics. On failover, standby is almost immediately ready — dramatically reducing recovery time. State store checkpointing: Periodic offsets checkpointed to disk — on restart, only replay from checkpoint, not from beginning.
questions.drawbacks: State recovery from changelog can be slow for large state stores (GBs of data). Without standbys, failover causes a recovery window during which no processing occurs.
questions.solutions: Set num.standby.replicas=1 in production. Use persistent state stores (RocksDB) to survive application restarts. Monitor state restoration progress via kafka.streams metrics.

questions.question: What is Kafka's exactly-once v2 (EOS v2) and how does it improve on the original EOS?
questions.answer: Original EOS (exactly_once): Each Kafka Streams task used a separate transactional producer. With many tasks, this meant many transaction coordinators and high overhead for transaction management. Also required zombie fencing via epoch management per task producer. EOS v2 (exactly_once_v2, default since Kafka 3.0): Uses the consumer's group metadata (consumer epoch) for zombie fencing instead of producer epoch. Only one transactional producer per Streams thread (not per task) — dramatically reduces the number of open transactions. The producer epoch is bumped by the consumer group coordinator on rebalance — this fences zombie producers without needing per-task transactional.id management. Result: Lower overhead (~2x fewer transactions), better scalability, and the consumer rebalance itself serves as the fencing mechanism.
questions.drawbacks: EOS v2 requires brokers >= 2.5. Applications using older broker versions must use exactly_once.
questions.solutions: Upgrade to Kafka 3.0+ and use processing.guarantee=exactly_once_v2. If running on MSK or older Confluent, verify broker version compatibility.

questions.question: How would you migrate a high-traffic Kafka cluster from ZooKeeper mode to KRaft mode with zero downtime?
questions.answer: Zero-downtime ZooKeeper to KRaft migration (Kafka 3.5+): Step 1: Upgrade Kafka to 3.5+ with dual-mode support. Step 2: Provision 3 new dedicated controller nodes with KRaft configuration (process.roles=controller). Initialize their storage: kafka-storage.sh format. Step 3: On each broker, add controller.quorum.voters pointing to new KRaft controllers. Set zookeeper.metadata.migration.enable=true. Step 4: Start KRaft controllers alongside ZooKeeper cluster. The migration tool (kafka-zk-migration.sh) copies all metadata (topics, configs, ACLs, consumer offsets) from ZooKeeper to KRaft __cluster_metadata topic. Step 5: Verify metadata consistency. Kafka provides a migration status API. Step 6: Once migration complete, set zookeeper.metadata.migration.enable=false on brokers. Brokers now use KRaft exclusively. Step 7: Decommission ZooKeeper ensemble. Producers/consumers are never interrupted — they connect to brokers, not ZooKeeper directly.
questions.drawbacks: Migration tool is relatively new — thorough testing in staging is essential. Rollback is possible before final switchover but complex. Some edge cases in metadata migration have been fixed in subsequent releases.
questions.solutions: Run full migration in staging first. Take ZooKeeper snapshots before starting. Use the --dry-run flag for validation. Monitor UnderReplicatedPartitions throughout. Schedule migration during low-traffic window even though it's technically zero-downtime.
